title: The mathematics of natural algorithms
speaker: Bernard Chazelle
abstract: I will review some of the recent techniques we've used in our study of natural algorithms. These include Dirichlet series for matrix products, mean-field approximations in opinion dynamics, graph sequence grammars, and tools for renormalizing network-based dynamical systems. If time permits, I will also discuss anti-mixing techniques for self-sustaining iterated learning. The talk will be self-contained and non-technical.

proof_stages.label: Bounded-confidence averaging model and freezing problem
proof_stages.summary: The talk sets up the Hegselmann-Krause opinion-dynamics model, explains equal-radius symmetry, and states known one-dimensional freezing bounds such as an n^3 upper bound and n^2 lower bound.

proof_stages.label: Stubborn-agent and nondeterministic relaxation
proof_stages.summary: A more flexible one-dimensional model is introduced in which agents may move only partway toward the local average, covering stubborn fixed agents, and convergence is proved by an algorithmic distributed-credit analogue of a Lyapunov argument.

proof_stages.label: Mean-field route to the 2.29R clustering law
proof_stages.summary: The empirical 2R conjecture is softened by adding Brownian noise, passing to a thermodynamic-limit PDE, proving well-posedness, and using Fourier perturbation/instability analysis to derive the asymptotic cluster spacing about 2.29R.

proof_stages.label: Kinetic s-energy for products of stochastic matrices
proof_stages.summary: The equal-radius convergence problem is embedded in a general theory of arbitrary products of type-symmetric stochastic matrices, where kinetic s-energy bounds imply convergence and are contrasted with one-step spectral methods for Markov chains.

proof_stages.label: First-order definable dynamic networks and generic periodicity
proof_stages.summary: The interaction graph is generalized from metric balls to edges determined by fixed first-order formulas over the reals, yielding piecewise-linear stochastic dynamics that can be Turing complete but, under perturbation and technical assumptions, almost surely have periodic attractors and can support robust long clocks.

proof_stages.label: Cell decomposition, orbit splitting, and entropy versus contraction
proof_stages.summary: Using Tarski-Collins quantifier elimination, phase space is partitioned into algebraic chambers with fixed stochastic matrices, and the difficulty is identified as the repeated splitting of sets of orbits across chamber walls, governed by a competition between contraction and topological entropy after factoring out dominant eigenspaces.

proof_stages.label: Message-passing renormalization of graph sequences
proof_stages.summary: To generalize the communicating-class decomposition of a fixed Markov chain, an infinite sequence of time-varying graphs is parsed by a grammar/message-passing mechanism so that spaces of orbits and their entropy can be rewritten via product decompositions.

proof_stages.label: Iterated learning as controlled non-mixing
proof_stages.summary: As an equilibrium-focused coda, iterated learning is modeled as a Markov-chain/Gibbs-sampling process that normally mixes to a prior, but a logarithmic increase in learning exposure can keep the initial information essentially self-sustaining.
